hero-description: InterVisions is a multidisciplinary project that combines intersectional feminist theory with participatory AI practices to address the biases and inequalities reproduced by artificial intelligence models that connect language and vision.
hero-goal: Its goal is to promote AI systems that are fairer, more inclusive, and aligned with fundamental rights.
key-aspects-intro: Explore the core concepts that guide the InterVisions project — from understanding how AI systems reproduce bias, to why an intersectional approach is essential, to the role of participation in shaping fairer technologies.
accordion-1-content: AI systems that process language and images are trained on large datasets reflecting existing social structures. These systems can reproduce — and in some cases amplify — biases related to gender, race, class, ability, and other axes of inequality. Understanding where these biases originate and how they manifest is the first step toward building fairer technologies.
accordion-2-content: Intersectionality recognizes that people's experiences are shaped by multiple overlapping identities and systems of power. In AI, this means that bias doesn't affect everyone equally — a system may disadvantage someone not just because of their gender, but because of the combination of gender, ethnicity, age, and other factors. Intersectional analysis makes these compounding effects visible.
accordion-3-content: Technical solutions alone cannot address the social dimensions of AI bias. InterVisions places participation at the center of its methodology — working with affected communities to ensure that the tools and frameworks developed reflect real experiences, not just technical criteria. Participatory approaches build trust, generate relevant knowledge, and distribute the capacity to audit and challenge AI systems.
tools-intro: Four tools created by the InterVisions Project, designed to integrate an intersectional perspective into different areas of action. Each tool offers specific methodologies for analysis, evaluation, and participation.
community-intro: Share your experience and reflections with us, and help shape the project. Your input directly informs the development of our tools and methodologies.
form-thankyou-text: We've received your message and will be in touch soon.
resources-intro: A curated collection of academic papers, reports, and tools related to AI bias, intersectionality, and algorithmic justice.
tools-page-hero: Four tools created by the InterVisions Project, designed to integrate an intersectional perspective into different areas of action.
tools-page-intro: Each tool offers specific methodologies for analysis, evaluation, and participation. Together they form a comprehensive framework for intersectional AI auditing and governance.
tool1-p1: We present an intersectional framework for impact assessment, designed to be useful and adaptable to other contexts as well. Impact assessment is a systematic process that identifies, analyzes, and anticipates the potential effects of an action — such as the design, use, or governance of an AI system — on people and the environment.
tool1-p2: The proposed intersectional framework goes beyond a simple technical risk review. It emphasizes how structural inequalities — related to gender, race, class, ability, or origin, among others — can be reproduced or amplified in the creation and implementation of AI technologies.
tool1-p3: The goal is to turn impact assessment into a transformative practice that questions and modifies these dynamics, promoting fairer and more equitable outcomes.
tool2-p1: The Audit Tool is designed to assess intersectional biases in vision and language foundation models. Its goal is to make AI system auditing accessible to individuals and organizations that are usually excluded from these technical processes, with a particular focus on those most affected by AI's impacts.
tool2-p2: Its methodology combines technical analysis with participatory and intersectional approaches, making it possible to identify how biases affect people differently depending on gender, background, age, or other factors.
tool2-li1: Co-designed with diverse participants, not only technical experts
tool2-li2: Includes an intersectional analysis to capture multiple and overlapping biases
tool2-li4: Focused on real-world impacts on vulnerable communities
tool3-p1: The workshops are co-creation spaces where directly affected communities and civil society organizations actively participate in designing the project's technical tools. Their purpose is to ensure that both the participatory auditing tool and the algorithmic justice benchmark are grounded in real experiences, rather than solely in technical criteria.
tool3-p2: Through a participatory and intersectional methodology, the workshops help identify how discrimination in vision and language models affects people differently depending on their gender, background, age, or situation of vulnerability.
tool3-li1: Designed with and for affected communities
tool3-li2: Incorporate intersectionality as a methodological core, not as an add-on
tool3-li3: Directly connect civic participation with technical development
tool3-li4: Generate collective knowledge and critical capacity in relation to AI
tool4-p1: The Benchmark is a technical tool designed to assess intersectional biases in vision and language foundation models. Unlike conventional benchmarks, which only measure technical performance, this one focuses on algorithmic justice: how models affect diverse people depending on gender, background, age, or other combined identity dimensions.
tool4-p2: Its main indicators make it possible to compare model behavior in terms of fairness and bias mitigation, and to evaluate the social impact of training datasets.
tool4-li1: Assesses intersectional fairness, not just technical accuracy
tool4-li2: Built from data generated through participatory workshops
tool4-li3: Directly connected to the Audit Tool for a comprehensive evaluation
tool4-li4: Aimed at becoming an industry standard
team-page-hero: A multidisciplinary team bringing together feminist technologists, researchers, lawyers, and community organizers.
team-page-intro: InterVisions brings together researchers, technologists, and community organizers from different disciplines and institutions, united by a commitment to building fairer AI systems.
member-ec-bio: Holds a degree in Psychology and a Masters in Public and Social Policies. Her career focuses especially on the intersection of feminism, technopolitics, and digital rights, with an emphasis on combating digital violence and promoting technological sovereignty from a feminist perspective.
member-mo-bio: A political scientist and lawyer working at the intersection of technology, law, and social justice. She contributes to the European InterVisions project through Donestech, where she promotes inclusion and equity in AI systems and develops methods to certify and document online gender-based violence and hate speech.
member-lg-bio: Investigador Ramón y Cajal at the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona and member of the Computer Vision Center (CVC), working at the intersection of computer vision, machine learning and responsible AI. His research explores the detection and mitigation of algorithmic bias in multimodal AI systems.
member-sl-bio: Her work sits at the intersection of AI research, education and creative practice: she collaborates on research projects, develops AI benchmarks and studies on dataset biases, and designs training and workshops on generative AI, inclusive learning and critical AI literacy.
member-sr-bio: Political scientist and doctor in Political Sciences from the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona. Currently coordinator of the Area of Feminisms and Equality at the Diputació de Barcelona. Throughout her career she has combined institutional practice, academic research and feminist activism.
member-ae-bio: Advisory Board member contributing expertise on gender equality and digital rights.
member-sa-bio: Director of Presidency Services at the Barcelona Provincial Council. Passionate about data and disruptive technologies as a driver for generating public value and improving public policies and services.
member-mm-bio: Professional career initially focused on accounting and auditing. Later spent 10 years in the central budgetary services of the Barcelona Provincial Council, and for the past 4 years has been part of the team.
member-ap-bio: Urban psychologist working at the intersection of social design, community engagement, and spatial transformation processes. She developed her career specializing in inclusive urbanism.
member-nv-bio: Professor of the Department of Sociology and Director of the Equality Unit at the University of Barcelona. Co-founder of Donestech and Fembloc collective.
member-dk-bio: Full professor at the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, associate director of the Computer Vision Center and ICREA academia professor. Leads the research line on vision, language and reading.
member-ya-bio: Leads the Fundamental AI Lab at the University of Technology in Nuremberg. Previously assistant professor at the University of Amsterdam and a PhD at VGG at the University of Oxford. Research focuses on multimodal and self-supervised learning.
member-ht-bio: Academic whose research intersects ethics of AI and gender. Represents Society of Gender Professionals with InterVisions.
member-gp-bio: International specialist in gender equality and social inclusion with over 25 years experience across policy, fieldwork, advisory roles and academic research.
member-ac-bio: Cyberfeminist, researcher, and founder of Donestech (2006) and Fembloc (2022), exploring gender-technology intersections and supporting survivors of Technology Facilitated Gender-Based Violence.
member-lm-bio: Biotechnologist and specialist in science communication and education at Computer Vision Center (CVC), focusing on the intersection of AI, social impact and open innovation.
member-mp-bio: Computer programmer at the Dabne cooperative, she works at the intersection of the technical and the social, investigating, from a gender perspective, how technologies and power relations intertwine.
member-mc-bio: PhD in Political Science. Currently a senior researcher at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center in the area of science, citizenship and policies. Her career combines academic research with institutional activism.
member-ma-bio: Research fellow funded by Yale University, whose work lies at the intersection of gender economics, artificial intelligence, and public policy. Her research examines how AI systems have gender-differentiated impacts.
member-nn-bio: Working Group member contributing expertise on AI fairness and intersectional methodologies.
member-am-bio: Received his B.S. in Data Engineering from Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona in 2022, M.S. in 2023, and is currently a Ph.D. Candidate in the Document Analysis Group at the Centre de Visió per Computador.
member-gf-bio: Participation coordinator at InterVisions and freelance consultant in participation, democratic governance of AI and human rights. Currently working on the transfer of academic knowledge to policymakers for different research projects.
member-ab-bio: Coordinator of participation in InterVisions and member of Radical Data, working on strategic and creative projects at the intersection of art, technology and social innovation.
privacy-page-text: This page will contain the privacy policy for the InterVisions project. Content to be added.
